paragraphs.body: The Boy in the Basement

They met under the same maple tree that they always did, just past the church on Center and Third street. Maddie arrived first about ten minutes early, spreading out their hand drawn maps on the ground, lining them up correctly next to each other, and placing rocks on top of each corner to hold them in place from the strong May wind. Everly and Carter came together about twenty minutes later, Everly beginning to jog towards Maddie when she looked at her watch, displeased, and Carter trudged slowly behind with little sense of urgency.

annotations.comment: I was immediately interested in this story. Who doesn’t love a “gang of friends” tale, right?

paragraphs.body: “The least you could do is let me know if you’re going to be late. This is the second week in a row,” scowled Maddie as she looked between the two boys.
Everly put his hands up. “It’s Carter’s fault. I had to tap his window for five minutes before he woke up.”
Carter only shrugged.

annotations.comment: Nice details. Love that Maddie is in charge of this group.

paragraphs.body: “Whatever,” said Maddie, still scowling. “We have business to get started on.”

They had spent every weekend for the last three weeks working on their new project: drawing a detailed map of one house in their town, belonging to Mr. Bayer. Last weekend they had finished mapping, having completed it with such detail down to exactly where the overgrown weeds were in the backyard and having drawn each window on the massive house.

annotations.comment: I think you can combine the next paragraph and this one a bit. The information is all good but the release of it is confusing. Probably they first decide that they’ve aged out and now they’re going to do this thing.
annotations.context_left: every weekend for the last three weeks working on their new project: drawing a detailed map of one
annotations.context_right: in their town, belonging to Mr. Bayer. Last weekend they had finished mapping, having completed it

paragraphs.body: Three weeks ago, they had decided they had finally aged out of playing sardines with the younger neighborhood kids when they successfully hid behind Mr. Bayer’s backyard fence for two whole hours without being found by the younger kids. They were twelve this year and were chasing their childhood bliss that was slowly slipping out of grasp. As their second hour of hiding started to approach, Maddie was first to bring up the issue.
“We know this town too well,” she remarked. Carter looked confused,

annotations.comment: You might also consider whether starting with this scene works better for you? Not a demand, just thinking through this story.

paragraphs.body: “Why is that a bad thing?” Carter asked. But Everly nodded at Maddie.
“I know what you mean,” said Everly, looking at Maddie. “We know these streets in and out. None of the other kids would ever be able to find us, especially if we didn’t want them to.”

“Especially by Mr. Bayer’s,” whispered Maddie. None of the other kids ever thought to hide near his house. It was the type of house that immediately made you unsettled to look at. It was a very dark gray color. All the windows always had their curtains drawn and were frayed around the edges. He had a big backyard, way bigger than anyone else's, but you could barely see into it because there was a tall fence that stretched around that had cracks cemented with ivy. Maddie once overheard her parents talking about how he had a son who would be about six or seven now, the same age as the other neighborhood kids that they played with. No one had ever seen him though, but they all imagined he must be weird like Mr. Bayer, so it was probably a good thing he didn’t join them to play.

annotations.comment: This implies that he’s maybe 40ish? Seems a little young to be the neighborhood crank.

paragraphs.body: Carter looked at Maddie and Everly with a twinkle in his eye.
“Why don’t we start a new game. Just the three of us,” Carter said. Maddie stared at him, confused.
“Well sardines isn’t much fun with just three people now, is it?” she scoffed.
“No, no,” said Carter. “Not sardines. A completely new game.” He looked up at the house they were standing behind and the fence between them and their new game destination.

annotations.comment: I would like a little bit more from Carter on this. What is the game?Also, would Carter be the person who starts this game? He seemed reluctant, even hostile, previously.
annotations.context_left: “No, no,” said Carter. “Not sardines. A completely new
annotations.context_right: .” He looked up at the house they were standing behind and the fence between them and their new game

paragraphs.body: And so, the next weekend they met under the same tree they had every weekend to play sardines, but now they went an hour earlier than the rest of the kids in the neighborhood so that they would be gone before the other kids met to choose teams for sardines. They walked from their meeting spot one more block and then turned right on fourth street where on the corner lie Mr. Bayer’s house. Mr. Bayer never rarely seemed to be home during the day which added to his mysteriousness. They pretended to be talking to each other about school as they side eyed the house to make sure no one was on the front porch. Then, they crept between the outside of the gate and the house next to his and got to the part of the fence parallel with the back of the house. They felt the ivy-covered wood to see if there was a way into the backyard from this side of the fence. At one of the back corners, Everly found what used to be a gate leading into the backyard but now was woven shut with the ivy. They realized they had no way into the back yard.

annotations.context_left: And so, the next weekend they met under the same tree they had every weekend to play
annotations.context_right: , but now they went an hour earlier than the rest of the kids in the neighborhood so that they would

annotations.comment: Is it mysterious for an adult to not be home during the day, though?

paragraphs.body: Their curiosity, and want for childhood adventures, kept them motivated the next week when the wore hiking shoes and gloves. Carter was first to climb up the ivy and over the fence, then Maddie, and then Everly. When they had all climbed down from the fence and into the backyard, they found only bushes and overgrown weeds. Everly, who had brought his sketchbook began to sketch one corner of the big yard, acting as though he were documenting a crime scene. The kids took their jobs of investigating this neglected yard very seriously. Investigating for what, though they weren’t sure.

annotations.comment: I’m enjoying their industriousness but I don’t know what they’re actually doing yet.
It feels like you, the author, also don’t quite know what the premise is here. (Which is okay! But now you have to decide…)

paragraphs.body: They went back the next weekend as well. Like the week before, they first checked that Mr. Bayer’s car wasn’t out front. Carter was about a third of the way up the wall when they heard footsteps nearing. Carter jumped down to see Mr. Bayer who must have come home earlier than normal. His sunken eyes and hunched figure stood looking at the three kids who were frozen in fear.

annotations.comment: Is this the same weekend that the story starts with?

paragraphs.body: “You kids,” was all he said as he shook his head, and walked right past them to where a bin was, behind his fence. He opened it up and grabbed what looked to be a soccer ball and then walked back around the corner to the front of his house.

annotations.comment: While I found this funny, it was also sort of a let-down. That’s it? (But then again, I do think it’s great that he catches them and is like, ehhh.)

paragraphs.body: And so, the kids learned to be more careful. The next weekend, which was last weekend, they decided to only meet under the tree to discuss their findings rather than go investigate more at Mr. Bayer’s house. They finished making their maps of his yard, and laid each paper, each with a different section of the yard, next to each other.

annotations.comment: The timing is off. The story starts on their 3rd or 4th weekend, but now last weekend is their 4th weekend.
annotations.context_left: And so, the kids learned to be more careful. The next
annotations.context_right: , which was last weekend, they decided to only meet under the tree to discuss their findings rather

paragraphs.body: “This is sort of stupid,” said Everly. “I mean we are never actually going to find anything interesting in that backyard, are we? Maybe Mr. Bayer is normal, just kind of scary.”

annotations.comment: You could consider having them have heard a rumor that there’s treasure or something buried back there? They just seem sort of aimless right now.
annotations.context_left: “This is sort of stupid,” said Everly. “I mean
annotations.context_right: are never actually going to find anything interesting in that backyard, are we? Maybe Mr. Bayer is

paragraphs.body: “No,” demanded Carter. “We are going to find something interesting. I’m sure of it”

This weekend, Everly agreed that they could go back one more time. Mr. Bayer’s car again was gone, and they hoped and wished he wouldn’t come home while they were there. They climbed over the fence and into the yard. They decided to spread out, each in a different corner of the yard, Maddie closest to the house, and Everly and Carter in opposite corners farthest from the house.

Maddie was looking over near the storm entrance, leading from the backyard to Mr. Bayer’s basement, when she heard a whimper. She put her ear to the storm door entrance, and the crying got louder. Maddie waved Everly and Carter over, and they put their ears up to the door as well.
“Sounds like a little boy,” said Everly.
“Mr. Bayer’s son,” said Maddie, eyes wide.

annotations.comment: Ah! Very dramatic. I would have this happen on the first time they go, maybe second. You don’t need to dilute the story by spreading it out so much in time.

paragraphs.body: “I bet you Mr. Bayer has him trapped. We have to save him!” Carter yelled, almost smiling with excitement. He yanked the storm door open surprisingly easily. It was unlocked. Inside the house, the basement seemed newly renovated. There were no windows, but the lighting was quite nice, and the walls seemed recently repainted. On the couch, just out of sight of the storm door, sat the little boy. He looked frightened at the three intruders. Everly quickly began talking.
“Hey. I’m sorry if we scared you. We heard crying and are here to help you.” The boy looked confused, saying nothing.
Carter chimed in. “Come with us outside, it’s much safer out there.” The three walked closer to the couch.
“I can’t go outside. Bad stuff will happen.” said the little boy at a volume they could barely make out. Carter looked at Everly and Maddie.

annotations.comment: I like the idea that they’re doing all of this to try to figure out what’s going on with the boy. Maybe they heard he’s buried in the backyard or something.

paragraphs.body: “Are you sure you don’t want to go outside with us? You don’t want to leave your house?” asked Maddie.
The little boy shook his head, “bad stuff will happen if I do. My dad told me.”

“Stockholm syndrome!” said Carter, but he could tell Maddie and Everly didn’t know what he was talking about. He whispered to them, “He’s grown a bond with his dad who is holding him hostage, so he doesn’t think he needs to be saved.”
“What should we do?” whispered back Maddie.
Carter had a twinkle in his eye. “We have to pick him up and carry him as fast as we can outside. Before Mr. Bayer gets home.”
On the count of three the three of them worked together to pick up the little boy. He began crying harder and harder.
“Stop. Stop. I can’t go outside,” cried the little boy as they carried him towards the storm door and up the stairs.

annotations.comment: I love this and it will work better if this is their stated goal.

paragraphs.body: Once they got outside to the backyard, they carried him over to the fence and set him down, Everly and Carter standing behind him to make sure he didn’t run away. Maddie gasped, looking at the little boy. He was turning red, hives breaking out over his arms and legs. He looked woozy and was breathing fast. Everly and Carter caught him when he passed out, the sun beating down on his irritated skin.

annotations.comment: Oh, poor kid. This moment is a little rushed — I’d give this a few more beats. Madelyn, I enjoyed this draft. You have a great premise here. I’m excited to see where it goes! As I mentioned, I love that the girl is the leader and that Carter is the reluctant participant, but by the end, that seems to have reversed. So perhaps you need to do something differently in your opening — less time on character and more on what they’re actually up to? I do think that they need a plan that they’re pursuing. It doesn’t make sense for them to just be poking around someone’s backyard (for five weeks!) without any vision in mind. I’d love to hear them discuss the boy a bit more or have some other reason they’re pursuing this. And I’d tighten the time frame — you don’t need this to be a month-long endeavor. (Also, why only on Saturdays?) It’s a fun story with a devastating twist, one I wouldn’t mind seeing the fallout of a bit more (maybe Maddie ends up sorry she pushed the so hard to do this?) — good luck on shaping it!
